speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm conducting an interview with Henry Wermuth, and this is tape number two.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. And I have to continue my story, and the next adventure was one evening I crossed the road. And homewards to where I lived, I had to-- so it was an-- an area, sort of roundish. Trees surrounded. And towards me came a very comely lady. And asked me a question in Polish. Now, in those days, a well brought up young man, I immediately took my cap, hold it like this, held it like this. And I still see the picture. And I told her that I don't speak Polish well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next thing she said, she asked me if I wanted sex. Now, that was something which I would have never expected coming from the lady of-- from the lips of a lady. And I was sort of shocked. But I was-- I rallied fast. And with the air of a-- a gentleman, I asked, [SPEAKING POLISH]. How much?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, five zloty. And by that time, she may have sort of noticed that it was a little bit too much for her prospective client. I looked at my non-existing wallet, and I-- I didn't have a penny to my name. And-- well, it wasn't there. And I said, I'm going home. She should wait a few minutes. I actually-- one thing I would say, she said-- what she said. She said in German. But first she approached me in Polish, then she said in German. And when I ask her how much, she said-- no, she did not say-- she said in-- in Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I went away, I said, I will be back in [POLISH] minutes, getting for my-- you know-- going home for my money. Anyway, sort of-- I didn't have any, so this was my-- those little experience of youth. Right. During that time, what was life like in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't make a lot of sort of strenuous-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we did not start anything because we wanted to emigrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We intended to emigrate to-- to-- to-- to Israel, to Palestine, not Israel in those days. Palestine or America. Who cares. Adventure. Life. I was looking forward to.

speaker: interviewee
text: But our quota came near very, very slowly. And one thing I do remember, of course. Of-- about two weeks after we arrived in Poland, we heard of the Kristallnacht. And you know what that? I do. What was your reaction to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, obviously shock because knowing that all synagogues were burning in Germany, that soon reached us that-- you know, the message. And under the pretense of what happened in Paris a-- a short while before. Because a young man called Herschel Grynszpan, whose parents also were deported to Poland. In revenge, he killed a-- a German, vom Rath, in Paris. And the Germans took that as a pretext, and looted shops, burned all the synagogues, smashed windows, and-- hence the word Kristallnacht, night of crystal or glass.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, this was about-- it was just 14 days after we arrived in Poland. In a way, I was glad to-- not to be there anymore. But the shock was almost the same. Now, when--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- sorry to interrupt you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did that news come through about Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I-- I can't remember that. I mean, this was probably powerful enough news that people still phoned each other, and maybe even it was word-- known worldwide, and newspapers wrote about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: In those days, no. But my relations, I'm sure they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had access to a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it didn't mean much to me at the-- at the time, you know. It was like-- like the daily news on the radio and the television, it-- I was too young to really be interested in daily news.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with yourself all day long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really nothing much, except for trying to go out with other youngsters and going for a walk, going on-- with a girl, means going for a walk in the afternoon. No money to go to a cinema or anything. And oh, yes. At one time shortly after this last episode with that lady, as I went home to the Kleinbergers-- that's where Mother and sister stayed-- my cousin, who was probably 20-odd years older than me, say, give me five zloty. I just thought to myself, I wonder what made him just give me that sum. Anyway, what I used it for. Well, I paid-- played billiards. Got rather good at that. And people may say it's the lost youth, and I would answer, if only I had a bit more of that. And since I was-- only the loser had to pay, I could stretch the money for quite a long time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And now I go straight on to the 1st of September, 1939. And that is the day war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Poland before war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: October '38--

speaker: interviewer
text: Till--

speaker: interviewee
text: 28th of October, '38, we were deported. That means the end of October, and the beginning of September the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about 11 months.

speaker: interviewee
text: So 10-- no. 10 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: 10. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during that time, did your father work, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: To my knowledge, he did not have any income at all, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he coping? It must have been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --difficult for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Relations. Yes. We-- I must switch back a little bit now. Before the war started, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my father was always coping somehow. Maybe he had some income, I don't know. But you see, food we were given by the relations. A few months before the war started, we rented a flat. And then we moved altogether again. It was in the-- who knows Krakow, Rynek Glowny, the main marketplace. Is it like a circus in England, it's called-- you know. Like Oxford Circus, something. But much, much bigger, of course, because there was a marketplace. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, where were we? You'd moved into this apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We moved to this apartment where I then-- I went again in-- to a hotel royale and we-- and again I played billiards. And I was more concerned with beating my partner at the time than the news the war had started. I remember rumors that the Germans had invaded, and the other rumors were that the Polish army was strong enough, and they gave the Germans a bloody nose. Which of course was my first experience in war rumors, which you could never trust. And-- well, within eight days they overrun Krakow. And three weeks, it took to overrun Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain what was happening during those first eight days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first bomb of the war-- I think it was the first bomb of the war-- landed nearby. And we-- when the sirens went, we all went into the cellars. And amongst the people in the cellars, you know, there are always big blocks of flat there. There was a so-called ethnic German, a Volksdeutsche. And well, he said nothing will happen to me. Whatever which-- whoever wins. And he was the most cowardly of all, the way he behaved. At one time, he shouted 'gas!' And we were looking, and everybody took out their handkerchief, as if that would have helped. And I looked anxiously at the crevices in the cellar, if the-- if any gas would be seeping in. But of course it didn't. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, that was more or less the memory of that particular-- the first few days. When the Germans reached us, nothing much happened because they consolidated first. And the first thing that did happen to Jews that were-- those who were recognized as Jews in the street were taken to snow-- to shovel snow. It was a very particularly harsh winter, '39. And at one time, I was caught. And my mother waited nearby. And I was giving-- I was given a shovel, and I was doing proper work. And the Germans released me very soon. There was-- there were more people than shovels about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did-- how did they catch you? I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just, you know-- they waved to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were just in the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Come along. And one-- at one time, I worked with my father. And it was 30 degrees below zero. And my practical father always asked me to have a lot of socks in the pocket. Because I had one pair of gloves, a-- a bit torn as well. And we had several pairs of socks, I could sort of withstood-- withstand the-- the cold. Where others, it was so cold that some people who touched the metal part of the shovel, the-- the hand froze to the shovel. It was rather painful to-- painful. Anyway, 30 degrees below. And what I had after, it was a sore throat. Speaking of a sore throat, I had very often in my life a sore throat. But peculiarly, during the war only once. I'm coming to the gate in Auschwitz. So the screw tightened a little bit when people were asked-- Jews were asked to wear arm bands, arm bands with the star of David. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at first, they could hear now voices saying, if it doesn't-- if it doesn't get worse-- in Yiddish, actually-- one can survive it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yes. Or one-- one can take it, rather, is the better translation. But it did, of course, get worse. We had to leave our flat soon. Ah. Now I have to go back a little bit. Before the war started, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jewish organizations send us to a place called Zakopane. And when I was about 10 years old-- yeah, about nine or 10 years old-- I had a very, very strong cough, and my lung-- my-- my lung was weakened. And this particular spa was very good for-- because it was in the mountains. The air for those who suffered like I did. And when the time came and the youngsters went home, a group of youngsters, I could stay a bit longer. My parents managed for me to stay a bit longer. And in those days, I met-- I met-- I met two other youngsters. One of them could have changed my life, and the other one in a way did. The girl could have changed my life. She was a banker's daughter, and they were going to emigrate to America. She introduced me to her parents. And I said-- if I want to come along. Of course, I was a youngster. I couldn't decide that. And if I had, well, my life would have been different. At the same time, coming to think of it, it was at that time three weeks before the beginning of the war. And I wonder if they got away in time. Anyway, the other person I met was a Polish young fellow, [? Janek. ?] He impressed me very much with his big bike, motorbike. He took me in-- in a side car, and went in the mountains. And it was very exciting, and at some-- at one point, I-- I swear I was about-- above a precipice when he took a curve, a bend. And now at this particular point, he didn't change my life. But I met him later in Krakow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was still winter 1939. He was buying gold and other valuables in Zakopane, and came to Krakow to sell it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now I have to switch back again in order for this story to make more sense. When the war started, all my relations disappeared. And where my father stayed, he had a factory of cucumbers and jam. And another brother of my grandfather arrived and took over the whole stock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: All in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- my father and myself-- had our first lesson. So I had my first lesson, anyway, in selling. And it wasn't difficult because the black market already had started. And in time of war, where everything is easily sold, you know, especially food stuffs. And that's my first experience in selling. Now, when that finished, that's about the time when this [? Janek ?] appeared with gold. And I asked him if I should sell it for him, or rather my father. And he said, yes. So we invited him to our flat. And my father sold what he had to sell. And was-- that was his first contact with the black market. Now, the black market was something which turned out the only-- the only thing to be-- sort of to earn some money with, because everything else you start, we weren't very lucky. And at that time, there was fewer and fewer jobs going, and fewer and fewer things to be-- to be sold or to be had. And people were still trying to-- the-- save-- make use of their vastly devaluing currency, the zloty. And therefore, everything-- you know, got a good price, as it does with the black market. And it was-- anyway, it was to the satisfaction of [? Janek ?] when father sold his-- whatever he brought. And he promised to come back a second time. But before he went back, he invited me to go out. He liked the drink. And I didn't like-- and I didn't drink. Alcoholic, of course. And he invited me, let's look for some girls. I said, OK. Let's look. Little did I know what he had-- what he had in mind. It was going towards evening. And at that time, the sons of where my father stood, the name was Schneider. They left suits. The younger sons left seven suit-- suits, and they fitted me. And I put on a white suit, and I went with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was summer's evening. No. It wasn't. What am I talking about? It was spring at that time. But already warm. A warm day. And we went to the-- on the-- to the banks of the river Weichsel in German. I think Vistula in-- in English. Yeah. Vistula, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dark. Only the moon was shining. And you could see people walking all over the place, and couples pairing up. And before long, you had to watch where you stepped, you know, not to step over couples. And I said to him, let's go home. But as I talked to him, he wasn't there anymore. He was-- I turned around, walk towards him, where he was already trying to pair up with a blond. And very disheveled one. As-- and while I was walking towards him, I saw a group of youngsters.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was dark, but enough to see that. Coming towards us. And I warned him. And he-- he jumped up, and not sure why. We started running. They're after us. And then we are thinking, why on earth do they run after us. I said, was it my white suit, or was it somebody's sister? She was certainly very drunk and disheveled. And who knows? Anyway, sort of-- they threw stones, and it-- luckily, none of the-- the stones hit us, and we could have been hurt. And it was just a little adventure. And I-- Well, was that one of your first encounters, with anti-Semitism, or do you not think that was anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it couldn't have been. Because I had-- ah, I have to mention that. I had the star in my pocket.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The arm band. No. He said, I find-- I know other places. And I said, no, I have enough. And I went home. The way home, I encountered a-- a group of people as quarrelling. Two suitors quarrelling about one girl. And she sort of-- I know it-- she wanted to go-- go away, but they pulled her arms. And one on each side, and it was quite a-- a scene. And noisy. And from the top came a bucket of water cascaded down the balconies. And I passed as quickly as I could. And I thought, ah. Let us see-- that's something I can tell my parents. And as I turn a next corner, minutes away from home, I nearly run into three German soldiers. And I-- sort of when you are in a shock, you-- you use the German word, [GERMAN]. [GERMAN]. Damned. But I-- I quickly sort of came to. And I said to them, follow me quickly. Some Poles are harassing my sister. And there were three of them. One was a bit drunk. And two of them run forward to where they could hear the noise. And it was a very sort of funny scene. I run after the Germans, because there was one behind me. And they had already disappeared inside the house. And I prayed the door to be open, and it was you. They went in. And suddenly I said, second floor on the right. Just sort of-- and it helped that in that moment, a big sort of shout-- a lady-- a lady screamed. And they ran, the musketeers. Those two musketeers ran up. And I sort of-- this time, now or-- now or never. I went in-- outside, and I saw the third German soldier is relieving himself against the wall. And I said, I'm calling the police. And that was that adventure. At home, I was scolded by my parents. And one doesn't do that, et cetera, et cetera. I mean, I didn't tell them the whole story, only part of it. And I said, well, OK. I won't do it anymore. [? Janek ?] said he would come later. And that was this episode.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were still living in the apartment that your father had rented?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and now the next one comes up here. The next one comes up, that my father-- now we have to eat. And we didn't want to start selling our own few belongings. Nobody does. And the only thing one could do is black market. And my father, having made some contacts, went out there. And everything went English pounds, dollars, very much wanted-- valuta currency. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] German Reichsmark. At that time, it was Reichsmarks. And at one time-- I mean, my-- my mother said, you know, I-- they didn't-- they never allowed me to go there because it was dangerous. Because at one time, they arrested people. And the Poles and the Germans found it was a good game to make money. They arrested people, took away what they had. And from then on, of course, people didn't carry anything as-- as-- you know, on-- in-- you know, they were-- met in groups at certain places, but didn't carry anything. You know, in case they'd be arrested. And one day, my father was arrested and he had on him 800 Reichsmarks. Probably a German sold it. But how it came to my father, I don't know. He was arrested there for six month. He got six month. He didn't give away anybody. And so they couldn't contradict what he said when he said, for instance, oh, well, I-- I had some zloty. I didn't like the-- the value-- devaluing currency. And so I thought, it's better to have Reichsmarks. But the judge said, well, that's forbidden. And he got six month. Now, he could--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was put into prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, still. Now, it's almost like half-civilized, because against what--

speaker: interviewer
text: What followed.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what happened later, this is-- you know, we could bring him food. We couldn't see him, but we could bring him food. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother cope with this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I'm coming next to. Now, we had the rear part of a very large flat. Three rooms and a kitchen. And we let a room to some people. But what we had from them hardly covered the rent. It wouldn't give us anything to eat. On top of that, they took in-- the husband took in the brother. It's a fellow who in my imagination looked like a hero, but he didn't do any heroic things. But he did something else for us. He organized-- now, hard to believe in those days-- card games. And they-- they played for high stakes. It's a game similar to poker, only with four cards. It's called-- called euchre. And when the kitty was rather high, and it was decided who would be winning it yet, he took a note away for my mother, who provided them with tea and cakes and whatever, what have you. And my father must have be sort of amazed that suddenly instead of worrying about us, his food got better, what we sent him. And we had money as never before. But as I say, the screw of oppression was turning. And then suddenly, we had to go-- they renamed the place where we lived Adolf Hitler Platz. And of course, Jews have to leave it. We were sent to a place which probably was later the ghetto. I can't remember the street name anymore. I can't even remember the family where we stayed. Well-- and at this family, I don't know whether they were voluntary giving us the place, or was it because-- well, the Jewish-- the Jewish community-- Jewish council forced them already to do that.
